does: This paper presents a comparative red teaming study of GPT-OSS-20B that systematically evaluates vulnerabilities at two distinct levels: the standalone model and the model embedded within an agentic system. The authors introduce AgentSeer, an observability framework that decomposes agentic systems into granular actions and components, enabling detailed analysis of attack propagation. They apply iterative red teaming attacks using harmful objectives from HarmBench at both levels and discover that agentic systems exhibit a fundamentally different vulnerability profile than standalone models, including previously unknown attack vectors that only manifest within agentic execution contexts rather than against isolated model instances.
connects: This work extends prior red teaming and adversarial robustness research by identifying a critical gap: earlier studies focused primarily on model-level vulnerabilities without accounting for the emergent risks introduced by tool-use and environmental interaction in agentic deployments. The paper builds on HarmBench methodology for structured harmful objective evaluation and responds to growing recognition that safety properties do not necessarily compose across system levels. It connects to broader research on agent vulnerabilities and emerging AI systems architecture, while drawing on observability and interpretability techniques to instrument complex agent behavior in ways previous work could not systematically examine.
enables: This research establishes a foundation for more rigorous agentic AI safety evaluation by demonstrating that standard model-level red teaming is insufficient for deployed systems. The AgentSeer framework and comparative methodology enable future work to systematically discover and categorize agentic-only vulnerabilities across different model scales and agent architectures. The findings point toward developing new safety interventions specifically targeting agent-level attack vectors, designing defense mechanisms that account for tool composition and environmental feedback loops, and establishing benchmarks for evaluating agent robustness. This work ultimately enables more informed security practices as the industry transitions from model-centric to agentic AI systems.
